title: Bug: Rapl greedily selects dram consumption for first numa node
author: weriomat
body:
I am currently messing around with a 4 socket system. While looking at the output of sensors I noticed that RAPL reports the power consumption for each domain separately, but only one value for dram (and possibly other subdomains).

```bash
[root@cpl powercap]# ./target/release/sio sensors
...
── cpu/rapl ──
  RAPL dram                                  4.1 W
  RAPL package-0                            52.5 W
  RAPL package-1                            57.7 W
  RAPL package-2                            50.3 W
  RAPL package-3                            51.7 W
...
```

Each domain reports its own value for dram

```bash
[root@cpl powercap]# ls
intel-rapl  intel-rapl:0  intel-rapl:0:0  intel-rapl:1  intel-rapl:1:0  intel-rapl:2  intel-rapl:2:0  intel-rapl:3  intel-rapl:3:0
[root@cpl powercap]# cat /sys/class/powercap/intel-rapl:*:0/{name,energy_uj}
dram
dram
dram
dram
37871987688
16813759539
31533870934
23087939276
```

As each `/sys/class/powercap/intel-rapl:*:0/name` is `dram`, the `SensorId` is only added once in the `src/sensors/rapl.rs`.

title: Feature Request: add version number in release archives
author: suvayu
body:
Is it possible to encode the version number in the archive files that are included in a release?

The present generic name makes it difficult to tell which version I have downloaded.

### Current
`sio-linux-x86_64.tar.gz`

### Expected
`sio-linux-v0.2.3-x86_64.tar.gz`

title: Last call for the 0.3.0 train
author: eous
body: If anyone has any code they want to get merged in try to get it finalized and ready for review. Thinking we tag the release towards the end of the week unless something exciting happens.

title: Feature Request: motherboard/AGESA sensor
author: emansom
body:
## Description

It'll soon? be possible to read the AGESA version from either dmesg (and maybe sysfs?), would be useful to expose this somehow and show in the dashboard.

## Info

https://www.phoronix.com/news/AMD-AGESA-Version-Linux-7.1

```
until now there hasn't been a convenient way of finding out your AGESA version under Linux unless rebooting into your 
system BIOS/UEFI screen or parsing the BIOS version and seeing if your motherboard vendor's release notes for BIOS 
updates make mention of the AGESA version/update. AGESA updates can be useful for new performance optimizations, 
expanding system memory compatibility, CPU security features, new CPU support, etc.

Finally with Linux 7.1, the AGESA version when detected will be printed to the kernel log. Thus with the Linux kernel 
moving forward on AMD Zen systems it can be a simple matter of grep'ing the kernel log, e.g. sudo dmesg | grep AGESA 
for quickly and easily determining your AGESA version under Linux.
```

author: darix

if you want you can also use the platform to build packages for more distributions. (e.g. fedora) also supports triggering builds on push.

title: Research: analyze linux kernel tools for valueable sensor data
author: emansom
body:
## Description

The linux kernel git tree contains a few tools like `perf`, `turbostat` and `amd_pstate_tracer`. Those tools may contain implementation details that could extend the usefulness of our current sensors.

## Resources

https://github.com/torvalds/linux/tree/master/tools

title: Time waste/sink/giant money hole: Easter eggs/Konami Code
author: emansom
body:
## Description
It would be fun to have some small easter eggs embedded within `siomon`

## Implementation idea(s)
- When a controller is connected (detect with SDL/libsdl) and the [Konami Code](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Konami_Code) is entered:
  - The whole UI turns into a 2D ASCII art side scroller with [Bryan Heemskerk](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7GikBxcyLQI) pixel art throughout
    utilizing libcaca or chafa for rendering
    similar to how `mpv` can use it to render video to the terminal
    implementing it that way allows for normal 2D render techniques
    which then in turn get transformed by libcaca to terminal output
    prior art:
    - https://github.com/TermTrack/TermTrack
    - https://github.com/ricott1/rebels-in-the-sky
  - ??? profit? more ideas below

## Possible hurdles
- For the Konami Code game:
  Running a game at 24 FPS or higher in a terminal, with proper frame/blanking pacing; requires GPU acceleration
  afaict GNOME Console is [now](https://www.phoronix.com/news/GNOME-VTE-Go-Faster) GPU accelerated by default
  not so sure about ~~Windows Terminal~~ and KDE Konsole
  Might require some upstream patching there, to ensure everyone has a fluid experience

## Disclaimer
Do NOT waste Level1Techs resources/time/money on this, other than a potential review once finished. I'm convinced there are willing contributors in the community that will show up eventually.

In fact, unsubscribe from this thread if you are affiliated with Level1!

title: Research: Explore how and when we need to package bump
author: emansom
body:
## Description

Rust has no stable ABI yet, and is a moving target. Both the Ubuntu and Arch packages probably need a package bump whenever the Rust tooling packages get updated in their repos.

Might not apply if Canonical guarantees a version pinned Rust that only contains backports, unsure about that since Canonical has been moving fast and loose lately.

Additionally, Arch is rolling release and has no stable ABI guarantees at all, and thus needs to be bumped whenever any hard dependency (possibly soft too, if it relies on header files) gets updated.

For the best user experience, we should ensure our distro packages are always functional and do not crash due to ABI incompatibilities.

## Proposed solution

- Explore what dependencies could break our application. Probably just Rust + glibc.

- Introduce staging packages that apply against testing repos for distros (testing repo for Arch, proposed repo for Ubuntu).

- Setup a workflow that utilizes [rebuild-detector](https://github.com/maximbaz/rebuild-detector) (and/or [arch-rebuild-order](https://github.com/archlinux/arch-rebuild-order)?) to run every night in a GitHub Action, against the testing distro repos and our staging packages. To automatically bump the package, and trigger a rebuilt and package update if need be.

- Setup another workflow that syncs staging packages to production packages, to be triggered whenever the distro specific testing repo packages that we depend upon are synced to the production distro repos. (needs some tooling? periodic cron -like checker against git/bzr distro repo sources?)

title: Repo Management: maintain clean git history, utilize fast forward merge strategies
author: emansom
body:
## Description

Currently the merge handling is handled by GitHub itself, this has some drawbacks:
- Rewrites git history with useless pointers all over the place
  See https://blog.esciencecenter.nl/fast-forward-merges-on-github-752f900f45e8
- Creates useless `Merge pull request X from Y` commits when git histories diverge/do not align between source and target

## Proposed solution

Adopt fast forward merge strategies described in the link above and here:
https://github.com/orgs/community/discussions/4618
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/60597400/how-to-do-a-fast-forward-merge-on-github

This can be accomplished semi automated by e.g. utilizing the following workflow:
https://github.com/marketplace/actions/fast-forward-merge

There is a potential supply chain risk there. Better to copy their code to our own GitHub workflows:
https://github.com/sequoia-pgp/fast-forward/tree/main/.github/workflows
https://github.com/sequoia-pgp/fast-forward/tree/main/src

title: Feature Request: ACPI CPPC, AMD P-State and Intel P-State information
author: emansom
body:
## Description

Both the AMD and Intel specific pstate drivers expose some useful information via their `/sys` path interfaces
e.g. like `hwp_dynamic_boost` in Intel P-State driver and `amd_pstate_prefcore_ranking` in the AMD P-State driver

Additionally exposing the ACPI CPPC table in human readable format could be useful, to show in a single glance which processor core is the most performant

## Resources

https://docs.kernel.org/admin-guide/pm/intel_pstate.html
https://docs.kernel.org/admin-guide/pm/amd-pstate.html
https://docs.kernel.org/admin-guide/acpi/cppc_sysfs.html

## Proposed solution

Implement new sensors for ACPI CPPC, Intel P-State and AMD P-State in `siomon`

title: Feature Request: UPower integration
author: emansom
body:
## Description

It would be nice if `siomon` queried UPower over D-Bus, allowing for calibrated battery reporting and battery status for peripheral devices (e.g. MX Master 3 mouse)

## Proposed solution

Rewrite the battery sensor to query UPower over D-Bus instead

title: UX: Show more stuff in dashboard mode
author: emansom
body: ## Description Currently on bigger displays, siomon shows a lot of unoccupied/empty space ## Proposed solution Expose more sensor data and/or have a split view showing both the tree and dashboard mode at the same time

title: Can't see schtuff!
author: ghost
body: I figured I'd give this a spin and to my dismay I can't see schtuff! heh. I tried --color never, always... no change. My main term is rxvt (un knee corn) (urxvt) where most things like graphs and borders are all just black. xterm is the same but my xterm background is slightly lighter gray so I can see a titch better but it's still bad. Testing with Sakura the borders and graphs are a lighter gray and I can see but it's ooogleh...but I suppose data over beauty but the fact --color does nothing and it's illegible isn't ideal. Problem with my terms? Problem with sio? You be the George...or the Frank...I won't judge (ahh AHHH see what I did there?).

title: Split into crates
author: AustinMReppert
body: The library code should be in its own crate. This will allow for GUIs and other utilities to use the same underlying code.

title: Storage includes virtual (ZFS ZVol) storage devices e.g. /dev/zd*
author: bugfixin
body: It seems like it would be worthwhile to exclude /dev/zd* devices (zvols) and perhaps other virtual devices (do md devices show up there?) from dashboard storage listings as they are already accounted for by the underlying physical devices.

title: CPU cache shows per-core values rather than full values?
author: bugfixin
body:
Shows: 
```── CPU ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  Name:            AMD EPYC 7373X 16-Core Processor
  Vendor:          Amd  Family: 0x19  Model: 0x1  Stepping: 2
  Codename:        Zen 3 (Milan)
  Microcode:       0xa001247
  Topology:        1 packages, 16 cores, 32 threads
  SMT:             Enabled (2 threads/core)
  Frequency:       1500 — 3832 MHz
  Scaling Driver:  acpi-cpufreq
  L1d: 32.0 KiB (8-way, 64 B line)
  L1i: 32.0 KiB (8-way, 64 B line)
  L2: 512.0 KiB (8-way, 64 B line)
  L3: 96.0 MiB (16-way, 64 B line)
```
Expected: 
L2: 8MB (512 per core x 16 cores)
L3: 768MB (96MB per chiplet x 8 chiplets)
I'm not sure if there's a reasonable way to multiply these out considering some are shared per core, some per ccx, etc, but it would be a Nice To Have

title: UX: start in dashboard mode by default?
author: emansom
body:
## Description

Currently the user experience for `sio` is not user oriented, more server admin oriented.
A example of this is when invoking `sio` without any arguments it spams the console with a lot of data.

Definitely useful for a server admin, but not a regular user that just wants to see fancy charts like `btop`/`htop`

## Proposal

Make `sio` behave more like all other monitoring tools by default: fancy charts (dashboard mode) first
Without requiring `-m` + pressing `d` on the keyboard

title: Bug: PPT shows GPU PPT instead of CPU PPT?
author: emansom
body: ## Description In the new dashboard TUI mode, PPT equals GPU Power, while my expectation was it would show CPU PPT? What siomon shows: What btop shows (focus on the `30.7W` part): ## Proposed solution Make it more clear to the user if it is showing GPU or CPU PPT Support processor PPT similar to how btop [acomplishes](https://github.com/aristocratos/btop/blob/main/src/linux/btop_collect.cpp#L978-L1017) this and/or from [SMU](https://github.com/amkillam/ryzen_smu).
